title: Electrospray ionization and collision-induced dissociation of antibiotic polyether ionophores
abstract:
Electrospray Ionization and Collision-induced
Dissociation of Antibiotic Polyether Ionophores
Dietrich A. Volmer* and Chris M. Lock
Institute for Marine Biosciences, National Research Council, 1411 Oxford Street, Halifax, Nova Scotia B3H 3Z1, Canada
A series of polyether ionophores (lasalocid, monensin-A, narasin-A and salinomycin) was investigated by
electrospray ionization (ESI) tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS). A fast short-column liquid chromato-
graphic (LC) method, using a ternary gradient sequence, was developed to separate the ionophores in less
than 4 minutes. Several mobile phase parameters such as pH and solvent additives were investigated, and
their impact on both chromatographic and mass spectrometric behavior was studied. The ionophores
readily formed stable complexes with various metal cations present as impurities in the solvents. Therefore,
a method was developed to convert the ionophores into only sodium adduct species prior to ESI analysis.
This procedure maximized both sensitivity and speciﬁcity in the subsequent MS/MS step. Low energy
collision-induced dissociation (CID) of the sodium adduct ions resulted in a number of structure-diagnostic
product ions for all ionophores. The proposed interpretation of these fragment ions is presented in this
work. Additionally, the fragmentation of a series of monovalent carboxylic alkali-metal ion complexes of
lasalocid was investigated under CID conditions. A comparison of the CID spectra revealed a decreasing
degree of fragmentation in the order [MH]  [MNH4] > [MLi] > [MÿH2Li] > [MNa] >>
[MCs]. To demonstrate the analytical potential of the LC/MS/MS method, its application to the
determination of salinomycin in catfood samples was investigated. Salinomycin was recovered from catfood
by a microwave solvent extraction procedure, and subsequently analyzed using LC/MS/MS of the sodium
adduct ions. # 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Received 29 November 1997; Revised 8 December 1997; Accepted 9 December 1997
Rapid Commun. Mass Spectrom. 12, 157–164 (1998)
Lasalocid, monensin, narasin and salinomycin are ionopho-
ric antibiotics produced by various strains of Streptomyces.
These antibiotics are used in veterinary applications as
coccidiostats for poultry and livestock and as growth
promoters for ruminants.1 Their basic structure consists of
a carboxylic polyether backbone (Scheme 1) which enables
them to readily form electrically neutral pseudo-macro-
cyclic complexes with mono and divalent cations in
solution. Complex formation occurs by intramolecular
hydrogen bonding between a carboxylic group at one end
of the molecule and a terminal alcohol group at the other
(Scheme 2).2 This coordination of metal ions facilitates their
transport across biological membranes, which is the basis of
the known physiological and toxicological effects of
ionophores.3
Toxicity studies have shown that the primary target
organs of ionophores are cardiac and skeletal muscles4 as
well as peripheral nerves.3 Many cases of severe poisoning
of poultry, horses and other animals have been reported.5–7
This usually happens when the antibiotics are fed above the
recommended therapeutic levels for a particular species
(therapeutic levels in chickens, for example, have been
shown to be highly toxic to turkeys and horses8). Typical
fortification levels of the feeds range from 20 to 110 mg/
kg,9 depending on the species concerned.
The wide application range of these agents in veterinary
medicine also represents a potential hazard to humans, as
residues of these drugs may persist in edible tissues.8
Furthermore, because of the presence of such drugs in
animal feed, agricultural workers may be exposed to the
ionophores when handling the feed, and could potentially
attain toxic levels.10
The need to identify and quantify ionophores in various
matrices such as feed, chicken eggs, liver, human plasma,
etc., is obvious. Numerous methods for the analytical
determination of one or more of these compounds have been
reported in the literature. Several authors have described
high-performance thin layer chromatography (TLC) meth-
ods for residue screening of ionophores.11,12 The majority of
analytical methods for ionophores, however, are based on
liquid chromatography (LC). Methods for lasalocid, mon-
ensin, narasin and salinomycin in various matrices have
been
reported with ultraviolet
(UV) detection.8,9,13,14
Almost all of these methods take advantage of a postcolumn
derivatization reaction between the antibiotic and vanillin to
form a colored complex (Komarowsky reaction15). This
derivatization is necessary because the ionophores do not
possess a useful chromophore. Chromophores can also be
obtained by precolumn oxidation with pyridinium dichro-
mate10 to give UV-absorbing ketone oxidation products. For
example, Karnes et al.10 described a reversed-phase LC
method for salinomycin and narasin in human plasma using
precolumn oxidation with limits of detection of about 5 ng/
mL. The high selectivity of this method10 was provided by a
heart-cut column-switching procedure.
The described methods, however, do not allow the
concurrent confirmation of a tentatively identified iono-
phore based on the retention time alone. Several specific
enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) for various
ionophores
have
been
developed16,17
with
very
low
*Correspondence to: D. A. Volmer, Merck KGaA, Central Research
Analysis (ZD-A/ZFA), Frankfurter Str. 250, D-64271 Darmstadt,
Germany.
CCC 0951–4198/98/040157–08 $17.50
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
RAPID COMMUNICATIONS IN MASS SPECTROMETRY, VOL. 12, 157–164 (1998)
 detection limits, sometimes down to the low picogram
level.18 Physical-chemical confirmatory techniques, how-
ever, are usually needed to ensure that such analytical
methods do not give rise to false-positive responses.
Generally, it is accepted that mass spectral (MS) analysis,
particularly combined with LC, provides unequivocal proof
of the presence of drug residues in biological samples.19–21
Only a few examples demonstrating LC/MS analysis of
ionophores have been reported so far. Blanchflower and
Kennedy22 utilized electrospray ionization (ESI) LC/MS to
detect lasalocid in eggs by monitoring its sodium adduct ion.
The technique proved to be very sensitive, with a detection
limit of about 0.5 ng/g.22 The same authors23 reported the
simultaneous electrospray LC/MS determination of mon-
ensin, salinomycin and narasin in muscle, liver and eggs
from domestic fowl. Using selected ion monitoring (SIM),
the ionophores could be detected down to the 1 ng/g level.23
The present work reports the results of an investigation of
lasalocid, monensin, narasin and salinomycin using electro-
spray ionization tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS).
Specifically, this study has four main objectives: (1) to
develop a fast LC method for multiresidue application to the
four ionophores; (2) to investigate the interactions of
ionophoric polyethers with a series of monovalent metal
ions under electrospray ionization conditions; (3) to study
the fragmentation of ionophore/metal ion complexes under
low energy collision-induced dissociation (CID); and (4) to
apply the LC/MS/MS methodology to determination of
ionophore residues in catfood samples.
EXPERIMENTAL
Chemicals
Standards of lasalocid, monensin, narasin and salinomycin
were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Mississauga, ON,
Canada). Acetonitrile, dichloromethane, ethanol, methanol,
2-propanol (Caledon, Georgetown, ON, Canada) and Milli-
Scheme 1. Chemical structures of the investigated carboxylic polyether ionophores.
Scheme 2. Schematic representation of the salinomycin/metal ion
complex (SalCat).
Rapid Communications in Mass Spectrometry, Vol. 12, 157–164 (1998)
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
158
ELECTROSPRAY IONIZATION OF IONOPHORES

graphic (LC) method, using a ternary gradient sequence, was developed to separate the ionophores in less

degree of fragmentation in the order [MH]  [MNH4] > [MLi] > [MÿH2Li] > [MNa] >>

by a microwave solvent extraction procedure, and subsequently analyzed using LC/MS/MS of the sodium
adduct ions. # 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.

ELECTROSPRAY IONIZATION OF IONOPHORES
Q organic free water (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) were
used as solvents. The alkali metal acetates and chlorides,
ammonium acetate and formic acid, were purchased from
Preparation of ionophore/alkali metal salts
The free acid form, the ionophore/lithium and ionophore/
sodium complexes were obtained by extracting solutions of
the ionophores in dichloromethane with aqueous solutions
of hydrochloric acid, lithium chloride and sodium chloride,
Microwave solvent extraction of feed samples
Extraction of the ionophores from catfood samples was
conducted via a procedure very similar to that reported by
Akhtar and Croteau8 for salinomycin. Briefly, 1 g of
pulverized feed was mixed with 17 mL of ethanol and
3 mL of 2-propanol in a polyethylene container and
irradiated in the center of a microwave oven at the highest
power setting for 10 s. The procedure was repeated three
times. The combined organic layers were centrifuged at
3000 rpm for 8 min. The liquid phase was decanted into a
100 mL round bottom flask and evaporated to dryness on a
rotary evaporator. The residue was then reconstituted in
salinomycin in fortified catfood samples at the 1 ppm level
were 98%, comparable to those reported by Akhtar and
The HPLC system used was a Model 1090 liquid
chromatograph (Hewlett-Packard, Palo Alto, CA, USA).
YMC/3-4-5 cartridge columns (4.0  50mm), packed with
base-deactivated 3 mm ODS-AQ stationary phases from
YMC (Wilmington, NC, USA) were used at a flow rate of
following ternary gradient sequence: the solvents were
A = water (2 mM sodium acetate  3.0% [v/v] formic
acid), B = acetonitrile and C = methanol. The mobile phase
composition (A:B:C) was programmed from 10:74:16 (v/v)
to 5:1:94 within 6 minutes. Aliquots of 20 mL of standard
solutions were injected by the HP 1090 autosampler.
Electrospray mass spectrometry and MS/MS
Electrospray data were acquired using an API 300 (MDS-
Sciex, Concord, ON, Canada) triple-quadrupole (Q1q2Q3)
mass spectrometer in the positive ion mode. The instrument
was operated at a spray voltage of 5.5 kV, a ring-electrode
voltage of 320 V and an orifice–skimmer potential differ-
ence of 45 V. Air was used as nebulizer gas at a flow-rate of
1.2 L/min, and ultra-pure nitrogen was used as curtain gas at
1.1 L/min. The mobile-phase flow was split, delivering
60 mL/min to the mass spectrometer.
MS/MS of the metal adduct ions of the ionophores, in the
product ion scan and multiple reaction monitoring (MRM)
modes, was performed using nitrogen as collision gas at a
pressure of p(N2) = 2.5 m torr (collision gas thickness
[CGT], 1.65  1016 atoms/cm2) in the collision quadrupole
q2. The collision-offset voltage, DVc, which determines the
laboratory frame collision energy, was varied between 15
and 60 V, depending on the adduct ions concerned. (DVc
refers to the potential difference between the high pressure
entrance quadrupole lens q0 and the collision cell quadru-
pole q2). Product ion spectra were obtained by scanning Q3
over the mass range m/z 200–800. In those MS/MS
experiments in which the collision-offset voltage was
changed, the inter-quadrupole lens potentials and the float
potential of the resolving quadrupole Q3 were linked to the
q2 potential, to maintain proper transmission through Q3.
Calculation of pKa were performed using the pKalc 3.2
prediction module (CompuDrug Chemistry/VCH Software,
Weinheim, Germany) installed within the Pallas-for-Win-
Liquid chromatography, adduct formation and
The LC method used short base-deactivated C-18 columns,
similar to those recently described for fast analysis of
pharmaceuticals19–21 and pesticides.24 Baseline separation
of the four investigated ionophores and two of their
derivatives, present as impurities in the standards, was
achieved in only 3.5 min (Fig. 1 and Table 1), using a
ternary gradient sequence. Simple LC gradients using
complete separation of all compounds. On the one hand,
compounds 4–6 could readily be separated with methanol/
water mixtures, but 2 and 3 overlapped in those experi-
ments. On the other hand, acetonitrile/water gradients
yielded base-line separation of compounds 2 and 3, while
4 and 5 were not fully baseline resolved. Therefore, a
ternary gradient sequence was adopted to take advantage of
the combined selectivities of both modifiers. Thus, the
acetonitrile content was chosen to be initially very high, but
was then rapidly reduced while methanol was instantly
ionophores and some of their derivatives. For peak assignments, refer

increased (see experimental). Formic acid was added to the
mobile phase to improve peak shapes. This acid addition
had the additional benefit of improving considerably the
spectral quality of the electrospray mass spectra (see
As pointed out by Kennedy and Blanchflower,22,23 the
ion intensity ratios of [MNa] and [MNH4] were not
reproducible in their LC/MS analyses. This was most likely
due to the high affinity of the ionophores for various
impurities present in the mobile phase. This can easily be
seen from the electrospray spectra obtained when no buffer
was added to the mobile phase (Fig. 2(a)). A wide variety of
different ion species such as the protonated molecule ions
([MH]), fragment ions of the very labile [MH] ions,
different metal ion complexes ([MCat], [MÿH2Cat],
Cat = Na, K, etc.) and cluster ions such as [MNH4]
and [MHCH3CN], were formed. Using the ionophores
in their free acid, sodium or lithium form as precursor
compounds (see experimental), or utilizing high purity
solvents for the mobile phase, did not substantially improve
Therefore, a method was developed to convert the
ionophores into a single adduct species prior to ES
ionization in order to maximize sensitivity and specificity
in SIM and MS/MS analyses. Although the ionophores have
a very high affinity for sodium, the exclusive addition of
sodium acetate to the mobile phase did not yield the desired
effect (Fig. 2(b)). While [MH] and the corresponding
signals from [MHÿnH2O] (n = 1,2,3) almost completely
disappeared, the formation of very intense di-sodium
species with all ionophores, as well as other adducts was
observed. However, by additionally acidifying the mobile
phase with formic acid, very clean electrospray spectra were
species (Fig. 2(c)). This effect was probably due to
protonation of the terminal carboxyl groups at low pH,

[MÿH2Cat] formation. Also, protonation of the carboxy
function could lessen the extent of hydrogen bonding
between the carboxyl function and the terminal hydroxyl
group, thus making cyclic complex formation more difficult
(see next section). Moreover, the presence of formic acid in
the mobile phase improved the peak shapes in the
The [MNa] ions proved to be very stable in the ion
source and did not undergo in-source CID under optimum
atmospheric-pressure ionization (API) conditions.
Addition of ammonium salts instead of sodium did not
prove useful. Although ammonium salts are more volatile
than sodium salts and thus offer general advantages as
buffers in LC/MS, the [MNH4] ions formed were very
labile and underwent substantial in-source CID to various
dehydrated species, even at low potential differences in the
transport region of the API interface. Furthermore, even
with high concentrations of ammonium ions present in the
mobile phase, abundant signals corresponding to sodium
adducts were obtained. A similar effect took place with
lithium salts. Although the formed lithium complexes were
more stable in the ESI source than the ammonium species,
abundant [MNa] signals still appeared in the spectra.
MS/MS of monovalent alkali metal adduct ions
As discussed in the previous section, the sodium adduct ions
were used for the analytical determination of the investi-
gated ionophores. To enhance the specificity of analysis,
MS/MS was applied for confirmation of residues in feed
samples (see below). It was therefore of interest to study the
dissociation behavior of the various metal ion species under
CID conditions. The CID spectra of the [MNa] ions of
monensin-A, monensin-B, narasin-A and salinomycin are
shown in Fig. 3 (the CID spectrum of lasalocid will be
In addition to neutral water losses ([MNaÿnH2O],
n = 1,2,3) from the precursor ions ([MNa] at m/z 773
fragmentation pathways were observed in the CID spectra
of salinomycin and narasin. Both pathways involved initial
-cleavage of the oxygen-activated carbon–carbon bonds on
either sides of the C(11) carbonyl function. As an illustrative
example, the proposed interpretation of product ions of
[MNa] of narasin is shown in Scheme 3(a). In the first
dissociation pathway, b-cleavage and subsequent hydrogen
migration occurred at C(9)–C(10), resulting in two major
fragment ions at m/z 531 and 279. These complementary
ions correspond to charge retention (via the sodium ion) on
either side of the broken bond (for the left-hand and the
right-hand side of the molecule, the terms Type-A (terminal
carboxyl group) and Type-F (terminal hydroxyl group)
product ions, respectively, will be used throughout the text,
according to a nomenclature suggestion by Kim et al.25 for
polyether antibiotics). The formation of m/z 531 was then
followed by loss of a water molecule to give m/z 513.
Similarly, the second fragmentation route originated from
bond dissociation at C(12)–C(13), resulting in a Type-F
production at m/z 431 (Scheme 3(a)). The second narasin
factor (narasin-I, [MNa] at m/z 801) exhibited the same
CID behavior, with a mass shift of 14 mass units in the
Type-F fragment ions (Table 1). Likewise, salinomycin
followed this fragmentation pattern. Product ions at m/z 531,
513 and 431, identical to those in the narasin-A spectrum,
were detected (Table 1). In comparison to narasin-A,
however, salinomycin gave a Type-A product ion at m/z
265 instead of 279, reflecting the differences in substitution
between the two analytes at C(4) (see Scheme 1).
The two monensin factors investigated showed a more
Figure 3. Product ion spectra of the sodium adduct ions of (a)
monensin-B (m/z 679), (b) monensin-A (m/z 693), (c) salinomycin (m/z
773) and (d) narasin (m/z 787). Collision energy, DVc = 60 V.
Scheme 3. Proposed principal dissociation reactions observed in the CID analysis of ionophore/adduct
ions of (a) lasalocid, narasin and salinomycin (the listed m/z values are for narasin), (b) monensin.
# 1998 John Wiley & Sons, Ltd.
Rapid Communications in Mass Spectrometry, Vol. 12, 157–164 (1998)
ELECTROSPRAY IONIZATION OF IONOPHORES
complex fragmentation pattern under CID (Fig. 3(a) and
3(b)) than did lasalocid, narasin and salinomycin. The
observed dissociation reactions were the result of openings
of the cyclic ether rings, as illustrated in Scheme 3(b). No
Type-F product ions were observed in our experiments. All
Type-A product ions contained a sodium ion, as was also
observed by Kim et al.25 in a recent FAB-MS/MS study of
[MNa] precursors of nigericin and abierixin, two
polyether antibiotics similar to monensin. The product ions
obtained for monensin-B (Fig. 3(a)) appeared at 14 Da
lower than their counterparts in the monensin-A spectrum
(Fig. 3(b)), due to the different alkyl substituents at C(16)
The CID spectra in Table 1 and Fig. 3 were obtained from
chromatographic peaks using the mobile phase conditions
described in the previous section; that is, at acidic pH.
Complex formation may be enhanced at higher pH values,
where intramolecular hydrogen bonding, between the
carboxylic group at one end of the molecule (pKa 2.7–
4.5, see Table 1) and the terminal alcohol group at the other
end, may be favored. It was of interest here to study the
fragmentation, under CID conditions, of a series of
monovalent carboxylic alkali-metal ion complexes of
lasalocid at neutral or slightly basic pH.
Figure 4 shows the MS/MS analyses of the lasalocid free
acid, the ammonium adduct and different alkali metal
adduct ions. A comparison of all CID spectra revealed a
decreasing degree of fragmentation in the order [MH] 
[MNH4] > [MLi] > [MÿH2Li] > [MNa] >>
[MCs] (compare Fig. 4(a)–4(f)). The fragmentation
followed the same general pattern as that observed for
salinomycin and narasin, viz. via b-cleavage of oxygen-
activated carbon–carbon bonds at C(11)–C(12) and C(14)–
The greatest number of fragment ions was observed in the
CID analyses of lasalocid free acid and of the ammonium
adduct ion, at much lower collision energies than those
needed to activate the metal adduct ions (Fig. 4(a) and 4(b)).
In these spectra, the [MH] ion (m/z 591) was accom-
panied by a structure-diagnostic Type-F product ion at m/z
355 (corresponding to b-cleavage of C(11)–C(12) and
subsequent hydrogen migration). Both these ions exhibited
several intense satellite ions due to successive losses of
water molecules. Another prominent Type-F ion appeared at
m/z 255 (also with additional intense dehydration peaks);
this Type-F product ion can most likely be assigned to the
product resulting from b-cleavage of C(14)–C(15). Further-
more, the ion at m/z 337, originally thought to be a
dehydration product of m/z 355, was more likely formed by
charge retention on the carboxylic moiety (Type-A) after
initial dissociation of C(14)–C(15).
[MNH4] ion (m/z 608) involved initial loss of NH3,
followed by the same dissociation reactions described in the
previous paragraph for lasalocid free acid. Consequently,
the species ([MH] and [MNH4]) showed virtually
identical CID spectra (Fig. 4(a) and 4(b)).
The MS/MS analyses of the two lithium
investigated, ([MLi] and [MÿH2Li]), were consis-
tent with the proposed fragmentation pathways (Fig. 4(c)
and 4(d)). Interestingly, the di-lithium adduct did not exhibit
dehydration reactions upon CID. Also, Type-A product ions
resulting from Li retention on the carboxylic moiety were
absent in the spectrum. This most likely resulted from the
fact that one of the two lithium ions already formed a
compact ion pair with the carboxy function, whereas the
other one was bound by inclusion complexation.
The sodium adduct of lasalocid (Las.Na, [MNa] at
m/z 613) exhibited a Type-F fragment ion at m/z 377,
generated via C(11)–C(12) dissociation, hydrogen migra-
tion and sodium retention (Fig. 4(e)), as described above.
Also, a Type-A fragment ion at m/z 359 after C(14)–C(15)
cleavage was observed. Interestingly, the Na adduct ion of
lasalocid proved considerably more labile than the other
sodium/ionophore complexes investigated (Fig. 3). This
necessitated a substantially lower collision energy than for
monensin, narasin and salinomycin if a meaningful MS/MS
fragmentation pattern was to be obtained (Table 1).
Virtually no fragmentation other than expulsion of the
Cs ion was observed in the CID spectrum of the cesium
adduct, Las.Cs (Fig. 4(f)). This could possibly indicate
‘peripheral’ ion pairing2 of lasalocid with the larger Cs ion
rather than inclusion complexation as observed with the
small alkali metal ions. That is, the carboxyl group is
probably the major binding site for Cs. Malfreyt et al.26
observed a sharp break in computed cation–ligand interac-
tion energies from Las.K to Las.Rb in methanol. A similar
abrupt change in molar heat capacities occurred from Las.K
to Las.Rb.27 The corresponding changes of both computed
Figure 4. Product ion spectra of monovalent metal adduct ions of

m/z 608 (15V), (c) [MLi], m/z 597 (20 V), (d) [MÿH2Li], m/z
603 (20V), (e) [MNa], m/z 613 (40 V), (f) [MCs], m/z 723

ELECTROSPRAY IONIZATION OF IONOPHORES
and experimental values from Las.Li over Las.Na to
Las.K and from Las.Rb to Las.Cs, however, were
smooth.26,27 Malfreyt and co-workers26 attributed this
theoretical and experimental evidence to a major structural
change between Las.K and Las.Rb.
The solution pH of the mobile phase did not seem to have
a major influence on the appearance of the CID spectra. For
example, the CID spectrum of sodiated lasalocid obtained
under acidic chromatographic conditions (Table 1) is
virtually identical to the spectrum from the flow-injection
experiments at slightly basic pH (see Fig. 4(e)). This could
suggest that the complexes have the same cyclic structure at
all pH values investigated in this study. However, it is also
conformation once they are ionized by ESI, regardless of
their (possibly pH dependent) conformation in the liquid
phase prior to electrospray ionization.
Application to catfood samples
To demonstrate the analytical potential of the LC/MS/MS
method described above, its application to determination of
salinomycin in catfood samples was investigated. Salino-
mycin was recovered from catfood by a microwave solvent
extraction procedure similar to that recently described by
Akhtar and Croteau8 (see experimental) and subsequently
analyzed using LC/MS/MS of the sodium adduct ions.
Commonly, guidelines for confirmatory analyses suggest at
least three structure-diagnostic ions per compound, includ-
ing the [MH] ion.28,29 Confirmation criteria for MS/MS
experiments are less clearly defined. As pointed out by
Schilling and co-workers,29 choice of diverse product ions is
necessary for a high degree of specificity in the MS/MS
analysis. In this study, three structure-specific CID transi-
tions, based on the dissociation pathways described in the
previous section, were chosen for detection in the multiple-
A catfood sample spiked at the 1 ppm level was then
analyzed. SIM of the [MNa] ion at m/z 773 and the
following MRM transitions were applied: 773→531,
773→431 and 773→265 (see Scheme 3). The SIM
trace (Fig. 5(a)) of the catfood sample shows several strong
interferences, especially at shorter retention times, and a
high chemical noise level throughout the entire chromato-
graphic run. The non-selective nature of the microwave
solvent extraction step utilized here (see experimental) was
most likely responsible for these intense chemical inter-
ferences. This could potentially cause problems when using
single quadrupole instruments for analysis. An additional
clean-up step in the sample preparation procedure might
therefore be necessary when using SIM for quantifications.
In the MS/MS traces (Fig. 5(b)–5(d)), however, no
responses were obtained for ions characteristic of salino-
mycin other than those at the correct retention time.
This study clearly illustrates the suitability of fast short-
column electrospray ionization tandem mass spectrometry
in the analytical determination of lasalocid, monensin,
narasin and salinomycin. The described LC/MS/MS meth-
odology offers a number of significant improvements over
previously published LC/MS methods for analysis of
ionophores. First, the fast LC method provided a baseline
separation of all compounds in less than 4 min. Further-
more, the addition of mM levels of sodium acetate to the
acidic mobile phase resulted in very simple electrospray
mass spectra for the ionophores, consisting almost exclu-
sively of sodium adduct ions. This enabled sensitive
analyses in the SIM and MRM modes of the (tandem) mass
spectrometer. Moreover, use of structure-diagnostic product
ions, generated by MS/MS, instead of the usual molecule
adduct ions formed in the ESI source of single quadrupole
mass spectrometers, greatly enhanced the specificity of
analysis. Finally, the MS/MS methodology utilized here did
not necessitate special sample clean-up steps during sample
preparation. Thus, a fast and very simple microwave solvent
extraction using ethanol/2-propanol could successfully be
